walkthrough.title: Unfold the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Paladins of the West Kingdom set up. First, unfold the main game board and place it in the center of the table. This board shows the action spaces around the edges, the four attribute tracks for Faith, Strength, Influence, and Virtue, the wall area in the center, and the round track along the top. Make sure everyone can reach the board comfortably.

walkthrough.title: Set the Round Marker
walkthrough.text: Find the round marker and place it on space one of the round track. Paladins is played over exactly seven rounds. Each round, you will draft a Paladin, place workers, and then clean up before advancing the marker.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Worker Supply
walkthrough.text: Now let's organize the workers. Sort all the workers by color into separate piles: red workers for military, blue workers for faith, green workers for commerce, and neutral workers which are brown or grey. Place each color group near the board where everyone can reach them. These will form the general supply that workers return to when spent.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Paladin Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the twelve Paladin cards. These large cards each show a unique combination of colored workers and a special ability. Shuffle them face down and set the deck near the board. At the start of each round, you will draft one of these to determine which workers you receive that round.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the King's Orders
walkthrough.text: Find the King's Order cards. These are end-game scoring objectives that everyone can work toward. Draw three King's Order cards and place them face up near the board so all players can see them. Return any unused King's Order cards to the box. These objectives will shape your strategy for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Outsider Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the Outsider cards and shuffle them. Lay out a display of Outsider cards face up near the board. The exact number depends on player count. Outsiders are characters you can recruit during the game using green workers. They provide immediate bonuses and end-game victory points, but recruiting them may lower your Virtue.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Monk Cards
walkthrough.text: Sort the Monk cards by type and create separate face-up stacks near the board. Monks are commissioned using blue workers. Each Monk provides ongoing abilities and end-game victory points, and commissioning them raises your Virtue track. Players will choose from these stacks throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Tavern Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the Tavern cards, give them a good shuffle, and place them in a face-down deck near the board. Tavern cards provide resources or extra workers when drawn. You will visit the Tavern action space to draw from this deck during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Provisions and Silver
walkthrough.text: Place all the Provision tokens and Silver tokens in separate supply piles near the board. Provisions are needed to pay for many actions, and Silver is used to pay off debt and fund certain abilities. Both are public resources, so keep them within easy reach of all players.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Fortification and Garrison Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place the Fortification tokens and Garrison tokens near the wall area on the board. Fortification tokens get placed on the wall when you take the Fortify action, and Garrison tokens represent manning the wall for victory points. Keep the Debt cards nearby as well. Each Debt card is worth negative three victory points at end of game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player their own player board. This personal board shows your action spaces, worker holding areas, and development slots. It is where you will manage your workers and track your personal progress throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a starting set of workers in a mix of red, blue, green, and neutral colors as shown in the rules. Each player also takes a small amount of starting Provisions and Silver. Place these resources next to your player board. Finally, deal each player a starting hand of Tavern cards.

walkthrough.title: Place Attribute Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes four attribute markers in their color. Place one marker at the starting position on each of the four attribute tracks: Faith, Strength, Influence, and Virtue. All tracks start at their lowest position. Advancing on these tracks is a major source of victory points throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place Score Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one score marker in their color and places it on position zero of the victory point track. Some actions award victory points during the game which you will track here, but the majority of scoring happens at the end of the game based on your attribute track positions, cards, and objectives.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a random first player and give them the first player marker. Play order will proceed clockwise from the first player. The first player marker rotates each round so that everyone gets a chance to draft Paladins first. Now let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Rounds
walkthrough.text: Paladins of the West Kingdom is played over seven rounds. Each round has three phases: first you draft a Paladin card, then you take turns placing workers on action spaces, and finally you clean up and advance the round marker. After seven rounds, the game ends and you tally up your final score.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Draft a Paladin
walkthrough.text: Your Paladin card determines which colored workers you receive this round and gives you a unique ability you can use during that round. This is the most important decision of each round, because it defines what actions you can efficiently take.

walkthrough.title: Your Worker Pool
walkthrough.text: After drafting your Paladin, take the workers shown on the card from the supply and add them to your player board. These workers, plus any leftover workers you did not use in previous rounds, form your available worker pool for this round. You will spend these workers to take actions during the placement phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Place Workers
walkthrough.text: Players take turns clockwise placing workers on action spaces. On your turn, you place one or more workers onto a single action space on the board or your player board. The number and color of workers you place determines how effective the action is. You keep taking turns until all players have passed because they have no more workers or choose to stop.

walkthrough.title: Matching Worker Colors
walkthrough.text: This is a crucial concept. Each action space favors a specific worker color. Placing the matching color is more efficient. For example, the Fortify action favors red workers, and commissioning Monks favors blue workers. Neutral workers can be placed on any action space, but they are less efficient. You often need more neutral workers to achieve the same result as the matching colored worker.

walkthrough.title: Action: Fortify
walkthrough.text: When you Fortify, you place fortification tokens onto the wall area of the main board. This advances your position on the Strength attribute track, which scores victory points at end of game. Fortifying the wall is one of the primary military actions and pairs well with Paladins that provide lots of red workers.

walkthrough.title: Action: Garrison
walkthrough.text: You place workers onto garrison spaces on the board, and you score victory points based on the garrison size. This is a way to convert workers directly into points, but those workers are gone for the round. Balance garrisoning against other actions that might build long-term value.

walkthrough.title: Action: Commission Monks
walkthrough.text: Commissioning Monks uses blue workers. You select a Monk card from the available stacks and pay the required blue workers. The Monk card goes to your player area, providing an ongoing ability that lasts the rest of the game and victory points at end of game. Commissioning Monks also advances your Faith attribute track and raises your Virtue.

walkthrough.title: Action: Recruit Outsiders
walkthrough.text: Recruiting Outsiders uses green workers. You select an Outsider card from the display and pay the required green workers. Outsiders give you immediate bonuses such as resources, extra workers, or attribute track advances, plus victory points at end of game. However, recruiting Outsiders may lower your position on the Virtue track, so weigh the benefits carefully.

walkthrough.title: Action: Pray
walkthrough.text: Higher Faith scores more victory points at end of game. Praying is straightforward but important. Faith is one of the four tracks that contribute to your final score, and reaching the upper positions on any track provides significantly more points.

walkthrough.title: Action: Attack
walkthrough.text: Like Pray but for Strength, attacking is a direct way to climb the track. The Strength track rewards military-focused strategies and pairs with Fortify and Garrison actions.

walkthrough.title: Action: Influence
walkthrough.text: Higher Influence scores more victory points at end of game. The Influence track pairs naturally with recruiting Outsiders, since both use green workers and reward a commerce-focused strategy.

walkthrough.title: Action: Develop
walkthrough.text: Developments can open up more efficient action spaces, reduce costs, or provide ongoing bonuses. These improvements last for the rest of the game, so early developments compound their value over multiple rounds.

walkthrough.title: Action: Tavern
walkthrough.text: Tavern cards provide various benefits including extra resources, bonus workers, or special one-time abilities. Visiting the Tavern is a flexible action that can fill gaps in your strategy when you need specific resources.

walkthrough.title: The Virtue Track
walkthrough.text: This is special because it can go both up and down during the game. Commissioning Monks and certain pious actions raise Virtue. Recruiting Outsiders and certain aggressive actions lower Virtue. At end of game, your position on the Virtue track scores victory points. Managing Virtue is a constant balancing act between the benefits of Outsiders and the points you gain from staying virtuous.

walkthrough.title: The Four Attribute Tracks
walkthrough.text: Faith is advanced by blue worker actions like Pray and commissioning Monks. Strength is advanced by red worker actions like Attack and Fortify. Influence is advanced by green worker actions like Influence and recruiting Outsiders. Virtue moves up and down based on moral choices. Each track has twelve positions, and higher positions award exponentially more victory points.

walkthrough.title: Debt Cards
walkthrough.text: If you cannot pay costs or take certain risky actions, you may receive Debt cards. Each Debt card is worth negative three victory points at end of game, which is devastating. You can pay off Debt during the game by spending Silver. Managing Debt is critical. Sometimes taking one Debt to enable a powerful action is worth it, but letting Debt pile up will destroy your score.

walkthrough.title: King's Orders
walkthrough.text: The three King's Order cards displayed during setup are public end-game objectives. They award bonus victory points for meeting specific conditions such as having the most fortifications, reaching the highest Faith, or commissioning the most Monks. All players can see these objectives, so they shape everyone's strategy from the very first round.

walkthrough.title: Paladin Abilities
walkthrough.text: Each Paladin card provides a unique ability in addition to workers. Some abilities give you extra resources, let you take bonus actions, reduce action costs, or provide attribute track advances. These abilities are only available during the round you drafted that Paladin. Reading all available Paladin abilities before drafting helps you plan your round effectively.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Clean Up
walkthrough.text: After all players have passed, the round ends. Resolve any end-of-round effects, return workers from action spaces to the general supply, and advance the round marker. Any workers you did not use stay on your player board and carry over to the next round. Then a new Paladin draft begins for the next round.

walkthrough.title: End of Game and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the seventh round is completed. Now tally up your final score across all categories. Score victory points for your position on each of the four attribute tracks: Faith, Strength, Influence, and Virtue. Add victory points from all commissioned Monk cards and recruited Outsider cards.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Continued
walkthrough.text: Next, score any King's Order bonuses you qualify for. Add any victory points earned during the game from garrisoning and other actions. Finally, subtract three victory points for each unpaid Debt card you still hold. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most remaining Silver wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the West Kingdom
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players. Each player will draft a different Paladin and take different actions so you can see how the worker placement system works. Follow along even if your player count is different.

walkthrough.title: Round One Begins: The Paladin Draft
walkthrough.text: Deal out Paladin cards for players to choose from. Player one picks a Paladin with mostly red workers and an attack bonus. Player two picks a Paladin with mostly blue workers and a prayer bonus. Player three picks a Paladin with a mix of green and neutral workers. Each player takes the workers shown on their card.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Fortify the Wall
walkthrough.text: Player one has lots of red workers, so let's use them. Place two red workers on the Fortify action space. This adds a fortification token to the wall and advances Player one on the Strength track. Red workers on a red action space is efficient. This is the core principle of Paladins: match your worker colors to the right actions.

walkthrough.title: Why Color Matching Matters
walkthrough.text: Player one used two red workers on Fortify and got the full effect. If they had used neutral workers instead, they would have needed three or even four workers for the same result. Always try to match your worker colors to the action's preferred color. This is the most fundamental efficiency principle in Paladins.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Commission a Monk
walkthrough.text: Place blue workers on the Commission Monks action space and select a Monk card. This Monk provides an ongoing ability that will help for the rest of the game. Player two also advances on the Faith track and gains a bump on the Virtue track.

walkthrough.title: Why Early Monks Are Strong
walkthrough.text: Commissioning a Monk in Round one means its ongoing ability activates for all seven rounds. A Monk commissioned in Round six only helps for two rounds. The earlier you commission Monks, the more total value you extract from their abilities. This compounding effect makes early Monk investment very powerful.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Recruit an Outsider
walkthrough.text: They place green workers on the Recruit Outsiders action space and take the Outsider card. The Outsider gives an immediate bonus of extra Provisions plus end-game victory points. However, Player three's Virtue marker drops down one position on the track.

walkthrough.title: The Virtue Trade-Off
walkthrough.text: Notice what just happened. Player three gained immediate resources and future victory points from the Outsider, but their Virtue dropped. Meanwhile, Player two raised their Virtue by commissioning a Monk. At end of game, Virtue position scores victory points just like the other tracks. Recruiting too many Outsiders can tank your Virtue and cost you significant points.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Mistake: Ignoring Provisions
walkthrough.text: Player one wants to Fortify again, but they have run out of Provisions needed to pay for the action. They spent all their starting Provisions without thinking about future costs. Now they have red workers sitting idle with nothing to spend them on. They are forced to take a less optimal action instead.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Budget Your Provisions
walkthrough.text: Provisions are the fuel that powers your actions. Even if you have the right colored workers, you often need Provisions to pay action costs. Always check what resources an action requires before committing your round plan. Running out of Provisions mid-round leaves perfectly good workers stranded on your player board.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Pray to Advance Faith
walkthrough.text: Player two still has blue workers remaining, so they take the Pray action. They place blue workers and advance further on the Faith track. Player two is building a strong Faith strategy, climbing the track toward the higher positions where victory points increase significantly. This focused approach is much better than spreading across all four tracks evenly.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Spreading Too Thin
walkthrough.text: Player three decides to use their neutral workers to take the Pray action, even though they have been focusing on green Influence actions. They spend three neutral workers and barely advance on the Faith track. Meanwhile, Player two spent only two blue workers for a bigger advance. Player three wasted workers on an off-strategy action using off-color workers.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Stay Focused on Two to Three Tracks
walkthrough.text: Spreading your workers across all four attribute tracks is a common beginner mistake. Each track rewards concentration because the upper positions are worth exponentially more points. A player at position ten on two tracks will outscore a player at position five on all four tracks. Pick two or three tracks that match your Paladin drafts and commit to them.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Visit the Tavern
walkthrough.text: They place a worker and draw Tavern cards. One card provides extra Provisions and another offers a bonus neutral worker. The Tavern is a great recovery action when your main strategy has stalled due to resource shortages. Player one now has the Provisions to take actions next turn.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: Ignoring Debt
walkthrough.text: Player two takes an action they cannot fully afford and receives a Debt card. They shrug it off, thinking one Debt is no big deal. But at negative three victory points each, even a single Debt card is the equivalent of losing a significant track advance. Player two should have either skipped the action or spent Silver to cover the cost.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Respect the Debt Penalty
walkthrough.text: Debt cards at negative three victory points each are the harshest penalty in the game. Two Debt cards negate an entire attribute track's worth of progress. Never take Debt casually. If you must take Debt for a critical play, pay it off with Silver as soon as possible. Players who ignore Debt consistently finish last.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Develop
walkthrough.text: They unlock a development on their player board that reduces the cost of future Outsider recruitment. This is a smart investment because it will save resources every time they recruit an Outsider for the remaining six rounds. Early developments create compounding efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Mistake: Drafting Without a Plan
walkthrough.text: Looking back at Round one, Player one drafted a red military Paladin but then ran out of Provisions for Fortify actions. The mistake was not considering resource requirements when drafting. Before you choose your Paladin, look at what resources you already have and what actions you can realistically afford. A Paladin full of red workers is useless if you have no Provisions to fund military actions.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Draft with Resources in Mind
walkthrough.text: When drafting your Paladin each round, do not just look at the worker colors. Check your current Provisions, Silver, and any Tavern cards in hand. The best Paladin is the one whose workers match the actions you can actually pay for. A Paladin with fewer workers that align with your resources will outperform a Paladin with many workers you cannot use.

walkthrough.title: Clean Up and Review
walkthrough.text: All players have passed, so Round one is over. Return workers from action spaces to the general supply. Any unused workers stay on your player boards and carry over to Round two. Advance the round marker to position two. Let's review what each player accomplished this round and set up for the next Paladin draft.

walkthrough.title: Check the King's Orders
walkthrough.text: Before you draft your next Paladin, glance at the three King's Orders. These end-game objectives should guide your long-term plan. If one Order rewards the most Monks, Player two is in great shape. If another rewards high Strength, Player one should double down on military actions. Ignoring King's Orders is a common beginner mistake that costs easy end-game points.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: Round two begins with a new Paladin draft. This time, try to draft a Paladin that complements what you accomplished in Round one. If you started building Faith, look for more blue workers. If you developed your player board, find a Paladin that leverages those new abilities. Consistency across rounds is how you build a winning strategy in Paladins.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have completed your first round of Paladins of the West Kingdom. You have seen all the major actions, watched three players make common mistakes, and learned the core principle of matching worker colors to action spaces. Remember: budget your Provisions, focus on two to three tracks, respect the Debt penalty, and draft Paladins that match your resources.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin Your Quest
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on Paladin drafting, track focus, and Virtue management.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all actions, worker colors, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Paladins players make. First, spreading workers across all four attribute tracks equally instead of focusing on two or three. Second, ignoring Debt cards until the penalty overwhelms your score. Third, drafting Paladins based only on worker count without checking if you have the Provisions and Silver to fund those actions. Fourth, neglecting King's Orders, which are free victory points for conditions you might already be meeting. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Paladin Drafting Strategy
walkthrough.text: Your Paladin choice each round defines your entire round. The best approach is to look at your current resources first, then pick a Paladin whose workers match the actions you can afford. If you have plenty of Provisions and want to push Strength, draft a Paladin with red workers. If you just commissioned a Monk with a powerful ongoing ability, draft a Paladin that leverages that ability. Also consider the Paladin's unique power. Sometimes a weaker set of workers is worth it for a game-changing ability.

walkthrough.title: Focus Your Attribute Tracks
walkthrough.text: The four attribute tracks reward concentration. The upper positions on each track are worth exponentially more victory points than the lower positions. A player at position ten on Faith earns far more than a player at position five on Faith and five on Strength combined. Pick two or three tracks that align with your Paladin drafts and the King's Orders, then commit. Your primary track should ideally be the one where you consistently get matching colored workers.

walkthrough.title: Managing Virtue
walkthrough.text: Virtue is the trickiest track because it moves both up and down. Commissioning Monks raises Virtue, while recruiting Outsiders lowers it. The sweet spot depends on your strategy. If you are going heavy on Monks, your Virtue will naturally climb, earning good end-game points. If you plan to recruit many Outsiders, accept that your Virtue will be low and compensate by earning more points from Outsider bonuses and the Influence track. The worst position is the middle of the Virtue track, where you get mediocre points from both Virtue and Outsiders.

walkthrough.title: Debt Management
walkthrough.text: Debt cards at negative three victory points each are the harshest penalty in the game. Two Debt cards negate a huge amount of progress on an attribute track. The safest approach is to never take Debt at all. However, sometimes taking one Debt to enable a critical action is acceptable, as long as you pay it off with Silver before the game ends. Always keep a small reserve of Silver specifically for Debt emergencies. Players who routinely take Debt and ignore it consistently finish last.

walkthrough.title: Early Monks Compound Value
walkthrough.text: Monks provide ongoing abilities that activate every round for the rest of the game. A Monk commissioned in Round one provides its benefit for all seven rounds, while a Monk commissioned in Round five only helps for three rounds. This compounding effect means early Monks are significantly more valuable than late Monks, even if the late Monk has a slightly better ability. Prioritize commissioning at least one Monk in the first two rounds whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: Neutral Worker Management
walkthrough.text: Neutral workers are flexible but inefficient. They can be placed on any action space, but you typically need more of them to achieve the same result as matching colored workers. Use neutral workers to fill gaps rather than as your primary workforce. The best use of neutral workers is to supplement a strong colored worker plan. For example, if you need three red workers for a big Fortify but your Paladin only gave you two, one neutral worker fills the gap.

walkthrough.title: Plan Multiple Rounds Ahead
walkthrough.text: Paladins rewards long-term planning more than most worker placement games. Before each Paladin draft, think about the next two or three rounds, not just the current one. Which tracks do you want to advance? Which Monks or Outsiders are still available? How much Provision income do you have for future rounds? Players who plan ahead draft better Paladins, avoid resource bottlenecks, and align with King's Orders more effectively. The difference between a good player and a great player is often how many rounds ahead they are thinking.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't spread across all 4 tracks equally — focus on 2-3
Don't ignore Debt — at -3 VP each, it's devastating
Don't draft Paladins without checking resource supply
Don't neglect King's Orders — free VP for conditions you may already meet

Consider the Paladin's unique ability, not just worker count
Weaker workers + strong ability can outperform raw worker count

Pick tracks that match your Paladin drafts + King's Orders
Primary track should match your most consistent worker color

Heavy Monks → high Virtue VP naturally
Heavy Outsiders → accept low Virtue, compensate with Influence
Worst position: middle of track — mediocre returns from both strategies

Keep Silver reserve for Debt emergencies
Taking 1 Debt for a critical play = OK if you pay it off quickly

summary.title: Plan Multiple Rounds Ahead

Plan track advancement, available cards, resource income
Avoid resource bottlenecks with advance planning
Great players think ahead; good players react to the current round

walkthrough.title: The Military Engine
walkthrough.text: The military strategy focuses on red workers, Fortify, Attack, and Garrison. Draft Paladins heavy in red workers and push the Strength track as your primary scoring source. Fortify the wall early and often to build your garrison bonus, then garrison for direct victory points. This strategy pairs well with King's Orders that reward fortifications or Strength position. The military engine is straightforward but powerful when you consistently draft red-heavy Paladins.

walkthrough.title: The Faith Engine
walkthrough.text: The faith strategy centers on blue workers, commissioning Monks, and the Pray action. Your goal is to climb the Faith track while collecting Monks with powerful ongoing abilities. Each Monk compounds the value of future rounds, and high Virtue from Monk commissioning adds another scoring track. This strategy has the strongest late-game scaling because Monk abilities stack. The downside is that it starts slower than military or Outsider strategies.

walkthrough.title: The Commerce Engine
walkthrough.text: The commerce strategy uses green workers to recruit Outsiders and push the Influence track. Outsiders provide immediate bonuses that fuel future actions, creating a fast-starting engine. The trade-off is Virtue loss from recruitment. To make this work, you need to accept low Virtue and compensate with high Influence points and strong Outsider VP. This strategy shines when King's Orders reward Outsiders or Influence position.

walkthrough.title: Hybrid Strategies
walkthrough.text: The strongest players rarely commit to a single track. A military-faith hybrid uses red and blue workers to push Strength and Faith while Monks provide ongoing bonuses. A faith-commerce hybrid leverages Monks for abilities and Outsiders for immediate resources, though you must carefully manage the opposing Virtue effects. The key to any hybrid is picking two tracks that share compatible Paladin cards. Avoid three-way splits because you will not reach the high-value positions on any track.

walkthrough.title: King's Order Optimization
walkthrough.text: Expert players read the King's Orders before making any strategic decisions and build their entire game plan around achievable objectives. If two Orders reward Faith-related conditions, a faith strategy becomes significantly more valuable. If one Order rewards garrisoning and another rewards Strength, the military engine gets a huge boost. Sometimes the Orders point clearly toward one strategy, and sometimes they support multiple approaches. The player who best aligns their engine with the Orders usually wins.

walkthrough.title: Development Timing
walkthrough.text: Developments improve your player board and provide ongoing efficiency bonuses. The value of a Development is directly proportional to how many rounds remain. A Development purchased in Round one provides six more rounds of benefit, while the same Development in Round six gives only one round. Prioritize Developments in the first three rounds, even if it means sacrificing a round of pure track advancement. The compounding efficiency will more than make up for the initial investment.

walkthrough.title: Adaptive Drafting
walkthrough.text: The best Paladins players adapt their draft strategy based on what opponents are doing. If two other players are fighting over red-heavy Paladins, the blue and green cards will come to you cheaply. Watch which tracks your opponents are investing in and avoid direct competition where possible. When a King's Order rewards a condition that multiple players are pursuing, being the first to secure it is much more valuable than being the third. Adaptive drafting is what separates experienced players from beginners.

walkthrough.title: The Round Seven Sprint
walkthrough.text: Round seven is your last chance to convert remaining workers into points. By this point, your strategy should be locked in. Focus entirely on maximizing your final attribute track positions, completing King's Order conditions, and avoiding any last-minute Debt. Save your strongest Paladin draft for the final rounds if possible. An ideal Round seven squeezes every last worker into track advances or garrisoning without incurring any Debt. The winner is often decided by who has the most efficient final round.

summary.title: The Military Engine
bullets: Red workers → Fortify, Attack, Garrison
Push Strength as primary track
Garrison for direct VP
Best with King's Orders rewarding fortifications or Strength

summary.title: The Faith Engine
bullets: Blue workers → Commission Monks, Pray
Monk abilities compound over remaining rounds
High Virtue bonus from Monk focus
Slower start but strongest late-game scaling

summary.title: The Commerce Engine
bullets: Green workers → Recruit Outsiders, Influence action
Outsiders = immediate bonuses + end-game VP
Trade-off: Virtue drops with each recruitment
Compensate with high Influence track + Outsider VP

summary.title: Hybrid Strategies
bullets: Military-Faith: Strength + Faith, Monks for ongoing bonuses
Faith-Commerce: Monks + Outsiders, manage Virtue carefully
Pick 2 tracks with compatible Paladin cards
Avoid 3-way splits — won't reach high-value positions

Align engine to Orders for highest ceiling
Player who best matches Orders usually wins

Prioritize Developments in Rounds 1-3

entries.definition: A card drafted at the start of each round that determines which colored workers you receive and provides a unique ability for that round. There are twelve Paladin cards in the game, and each one shows a different combination of red, blue, green, and neutral workers.

On your turn, place one or more workers from your pool onto a single action space on the board or your player board. The number and color of workers determines the action's effectiveness. You continue placing until you choose to pass or run out of workers.

Each action space favors a specific worker color: red for military, blue for faith, green for commerce. Placing the matching color is more efficient. Neutral workers can be placed on any space but require more workers for the same effect.

Four tracks on the main board: Faith, Strength, Influence, and Virtue. Each has twelve positions. Higher positions award exponentially more victory points at end of game. Advancing on tracks is the primary way to score.

Each of the seven rounds has three phases: (1) Draft a Paladin card and receive workers, (2) Take turns placing workers on action spaces, (3) Clean up — return workers to supply, advance round marker. Unused workers carry over to the next round.
Military-colored worker. Most efficient on military action spaces including Fortify, Garrison, and Attack. Advances the Strength attribute track.
Faith-colored worker. Most efficient on religious action spaces including Commission Monks and Pray. Advances the Faith attribute track.
Commerce-colored worker. Most efficient on commerce action spaces including Recruit Outsiders and Influence. Advances the Influence attribute track.

A brown or grey worker that can be placed on any action space regardless of its favored color. Neutral workers are flexible but inefficient — you typically need more of them to achieve the same result as matching colored workers.

Your available workers for the current round. Composed of workers from your drafted Paladin card plus any unused workers carried over from previous rounds. Workers return to the general supply after being placed on action spaces during cleanup.
A military action using red workers. Place fortification tokens onto the wall area of the main board. Advances the Strength attribute track. Fortifications may count toward King's Order objectives.

An action that commits workers to man the wall. Scores victory points based on the garrison size. Workers used for garrisoning are spent for the round. Converts workers directly into immediate points.

A religious action using blue workers. Select a Monk card from the available stacks. The Monk provides an ongoing ability for the rest of the game and end-game victory points. Advances Faith track and raises Virtue.

A commerce action using green workers. Select an Outsider card from the display. Outsiders give immediate bonuses (resources, workers, track advances) plus end-game victory points. May lower Virtue track position.
A religious action using blue workers to advance the Faith attribute track. Straightforward track advancement with no additional effects. Higher Faith scores more VP at end of game.
A military action using red workers to advance the Strength attribute track and gain combat-related bonuses. Pairs naturally with Fortify and Garrison in a military strategy.
A commerce action using green workers to advance the Influence attribute track. Higher Influence scores more VP at end of game. Pairs naturally with Outsider recruitment in a commerce strategy.

An action to improve your player board or unlock new abilities. Developments provide ongoing efficiency bonuses that last for the rest of the game. More valuable when purchased early due to compounding returns.

An action to draw Tavern cards from the deck. Tavern cards provide various benefits including extra resources, bonus workers, or one-time abilities. A flexible recovery action when your main strategy has stalled.

One of four attribute tracks. Advanced by blue worker actions (Pray, Commission Monks). Has twelve positions with higher positions scoring exponentially more VP. Faith-focused strategies pair well with Monk commissioning and high Virtue.

One of four attribute tracks. Advanced by red worker actions (Attack, Fortify). Has twelve positions with higher positions scoring exponentially more VP. Strength-focused strategies pair well with garrisoning and wall fortification.

One of four attribute tracks. Advanced by green worker actions (Influence, Recruit Outsiders). Has twelve positions with higher positions scoring exponentially more VP. Influence-focused strategies pair well with Outsider recruitment.

A special attribute track that moves both up and down. Raised by commissioning Monks and certain pious actions. Lowered by recruiting Outsiders and certain aggressive actions. Position scores VP at end of game. Managing Virtue requires balancing Monk and Outsider strategies.

Each attribute track has twelve positions numbered from the starting position to the maximum. The VP awarded increases as you climb higher, with top positions worth significantly more than middle positions. This is why focusing on two or three tracks beats spreading across all four.

A card acquired through the Commission Monks action. Each Monk provides an ongoing ability that activates every round for the rest of the game, plus printed victory points scored at game end. Monk abilities stack, making multiple Monks increasingly powerful.

A card acquired through the Recruit Outsiders action. Each Outsider provides an immediate one-time bonus (resources, workers, track advances) plus printed victory points at game end. Recruiting Outsiders typically lowers Virtue.

An end-game scoring objective visible to all players from the start. Three King's Orders are displayed each game. They award bonus VP for meeting specific conditions such as most fortifications, highest Faith, or most commissioned Monks. Should guide strategic decisions from Round 1.

A card drawn from the Tavern deck via the Tavern action. Provides various one-time benefits such as extra Provisions, Silver, workers, or special abilities. A flexible option for filling resource gaps.

A resource token required to pay for many actions throughout the game. Running out of Provisions mid-round can leave workers stranded without actions to take. Always budget Provisions across your planned actions for the round.

A resource token used to pay off Debt cards and fund certain abilities. Keep a reserve of Silver for Debt emergencies. Silver remaining at end of game serves as the tiebreaker if players have equal VP.

A penalty card worth negative three victory points at end of game. Received when you cannot pay costs or take certain risky actions. Can be paid off during the game by spending Silver. Two Debt cards negate an entire attribute track's worth of progress.

A token placed on the wall area of the main board when you take the Fortify action. Fortifications contribute to wall building and may count toward King's Order objectives. Fortifying advances the Strength track.
A token representing workers committed to manning the wall. Garrisoning converts workers directly into victory points based on garrison size. Workers used for garrisoning are spent for the round.

Each player's personal board showing action spaces, worker holding areas, and development slots. Developments unlock improved action spaces or ongoing bonuses. Workers are stored here between placement turns.

The central game board showing action spaces around the edges, the four attribute tracks, the wall area for fortification and garrison, and the round track. All players interact with this shared board.
A track on the main board marking the current round (one through seven). The round marker advances at the end of each round during cleanup. The game ends when the marker passes position seven.

The central area of the main board where fortification tokens are placed via the Fortify action and garrison tokens are placed via the Garrison action. Building and defending the wall is central to the military strategy.

An improvement to your player board purchased via the Develop action. Developments may reduce action costs, unlock new action spaces, or provide ongoing resource generation. More valuable when purchased early due to compounding returns over remaining rounds.

After Round 7, tally VP from: Faith track + Strength track + Influence track + Virtue track + Monk VP + Outsider VP + King's Orders bonuses + Garrison VP earned during game - 3 VP per unpaid Debt. Highest total wins; tiebreak = most remaining Silver.

Victory points earned based on your final position on each attribute track. Higher positions are worth exponentially more, creating a strong incentive to focus on fewer tracks and push them as high as possible.
Victory points printed on each commissioned Monk card. Added to your final score at game end. Monks score both their printed VP and the value of their ongoing ability used throughout the game.
Victory points printed on each recruited Outsider card. Added to your final score at game end. Outsiders score their printed VP in addition to the immediate bonuses received when recruited.

Bonus victory points awarded at game end for meeting King's Order conditions. All three Orders are public, so all players can strategize around them. Aligning your engine with multiple Orders is a strong winning strategy.

Each unpaid Debt card subtracts three victory points from your final score. This is the harshest penalty in the game. Two Debt cards equal negative six VP, which can be the difference between winning and losing.
